\begin{questions}
\question[10]
  Heun's method is a Runge-Kutta method with the following Butcher tableau:
\begin{equation*}
            \begin{array}{c|cc}
              0   &      &     \\[0.5em]
              1/3   & 1/3  &     \\[0.5em]
              \hline
                  & 2/3 & 2/3 \\
            \end{array}
\end{equation*}

Consider the IVP $u' = -u, u(0) = 1$.

The method is stable for a given step size $h$ if $|u_n|$ does not grow without bound as $n \to \infty$.

\begin{parts}
\part Find some $h > 0$ for which the numerical solution is stable.
\part Find some $h > 0$ for which the numerical solution is unstable.
\end{parts}

Show all your work.

\begin{solution}
For $u' = -u$, apply the Runge-Kutta method:
\begin{align*}
k_1 &= -u_n \\
k_2 &= -(u_n + hk_1/3) = -u_n(1 - h/3) \\
u_{n+1} &= u_n + h\left(\frac{2}{3}k_1 + \frac{2}{3}k_2\right) \\
&= u_n + \frac{2h}{3}[-u_n - u_n(1-h/3)] \\
&= u_n\left(1 - \frac{4h}{3} + \frac{2h^2}{9}\right)
\end{align*}

The growth factor is $R(h) = 1 - \frac{4h}{3} + \frac{2h^2}{9}$.

Stability requires $|R(h)| \leq 1$.

Check values of $h$ by trial and error analysis. Here are two examples:

\textbf{Part (a):} Let $h = 1$. Then:
$$R(1) = 1 - \frac{4}{3} + \frac{2}{9} = \frac{9 - 12 + 2}{9} = -\frac{1}{9}$$
Since $|R(1)| = 1/9 < 1$, the method is \textbf{stable} for $h = 1$.

\textbf{Part (b):} 
Try $h = 7$:
$$R(7) = 1 - \frac{28}{3} + \frac{98}{9} = \frac{9 - 84 + 98}{9} = \frac{23}{9}$$
Since $|R(7)| = 23/9 \approx 2.56 > 1$, the method is \textbf{unstable} for $h = 7$.
\end{solution}

\newpage 

\question[5]
Find the Butcher tableau for the following Runge-Kutta method:
\begin{align*}
k_1 &= hf(t_n, y_n)\\
k_2 &= hf\!\left(t_n + \tfrac{h}{3},\; y_n + \tfrac{1}{3} k_1\right)\\
k_3 &= hf\!\left(t_n + \tfrac{2h}{3},\; y_n - \tfrac{1}{3} k_1 +  k_2\right)\\
k_4 &= hf(t_n + h,\; y_n +  k_1 -  k_2 +  k_3)\\
y_{n+1} &= y_n + \left(\tfrac{1}{8}k_1 + \tfrac{3}{8}k_2 + \tfrac{3}{8}k_3 + \tfrac{1}{8}k_4\right)
\end{align*}

\begin{solution}
The Butcher tableau is:
\begin{equation*}
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
0   & 0    & 0    & 0   & 0   \\[0.5em]
1/3 & 1/3  & 0    & 0   & 0   \\[0.5em]
2/3 & -1/3 & 1    & 0   & 0   \\[0.5em]
1   & 1    & -1   & 1   & 0   \\[0.5em]
\hline
    & 1/8  & 3/8  & 3/8 & 1/8 \\
\end{array}
\end{equation*}

This is the classical fourth-order Runge-Kutta method (RK4).
\end{solution}

\end{questions}
